title: Red Wine on Polyester
stainChemistry: Red wine contains anthocyanins (red-purple pigments), condensed tannins, and tartaric acid. Anthocyanins bond to polyester immediately through hydrogen bonding and color intensifies with pH changes — alkaline conditions turn wine stains blue-purple and more difficult to remove. The tannin component oxidizes and darkens over time, cross-linking with the fiber polymer.
whyThisWorks: The acidic tannin spotter maintains correct pH (acidic) which keeps anthocyanins in their most removable red form on polyester. The oxidizer then breaks the chromophore bonds in the anthocyanin molecule. Avoiding alkaline products is critical — ammonia, baking soda, and alkaline detergents permanently darken wine stains by shifting the anthocyanin into its blue form.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply Tannin Formula (acidic tannin spotter) and tamp gently. Work from edge to center
2. Apply H2O2 6% (professional oxidizer) to break down color-causing bonds. Monitor every 5 min
3. Flush with NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) and leveling agent to remove broken-down tannin
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse
warnings: NEVER use ammonia or alkaline products on tannin stains — permanently darkens the color

homeSolutions.agent: Polyester rule: surface-bound, no high heat
homeSolutions.instruction: Polyester doesn't absorb liquid the way cotton does — red wine sits on the fiber surface and bonds weakly through hydrogen bonds. Good news: the stain is shallower than it looks. Bad news: polyester is heat-sensitive and dye-oleophilic. Never use hot water (melts/shrinks the fiber), never tumble-dry until the stain is fully gone (heat fuses the pigment into the fiber).

homeSolutions.agent: Patch test on a hidden seam
homeSolutions.instruction: On an inside seam, dab a drop of Dawn + 3% peroxide. Blot with a white cloth after 60 seconds. Most polyester is colorfast but some cheap fast-fashion dye lifts under peroxide. If dye transfers, stop and go to step 8.

Red wine is a tannin — anthocyanins plus polyphenols. Alkaline products (ammonia, baking soda, OxiClean on untreated tannin, most all-purpose sprays) permanently lock and darken the color. Use cold water, Dawn, 3% peroxide, and white vinegar only. Salt is neutral — doesn't help or hurt.

Turn the fabric inside out and run cold water through the BACK of the stain for 30–60 seconds. Polyester's low absorbency means a surface stain often rinses nearly out just with cold water pressure from the back. Skip warm or hot — polyester is heat-sensitive and warm water drives tannin into the fiber.

Mix one tablespoon of Dawn dish soap with one cup of 3% hydrogen peroxide in a bowl. Dip a white cloth, wring nearly dry, dab the stain — outside edges inward. Let sit 5–10 minutes, blot with a clean damp cloth. Polyester stains usually lift faster than cotton because the stain is on the fiber surface rather than absorbed deep. 1–2 cycles usually enough.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with ammonia or bleach — gas. On brightly colored polyester, the step-2 patch test is your safeguard.

homeSolutions.instruction: Mix one tablespoon of white vinegar with one cup of cold water. Dab onto the treated spot with a clean white cloth. Neutralizes peroxide residue and keeps residual tannin in its lighter form. Blot dry with a fresh cloth.

Launder on cold with your normal detergent. Hang or lay flat to air-dry. DO NOT tumble-dry until you've inspected in daylight. Dryer heat on residual wine locks the pigment into polyester permanently — polyester fuses pigment at temperatures lower than cotton's tolerance.

If the stain has been through a hot dryer, sat for days, or survived two home rounds, take it in. Tell them: "Red wine on polyester, age [fresh / days / heat-set], treated with Dawn + 3% peroxide + vinegar rinse. No alkaline products, no hot water." Pros use commercial tannin formula + 6% peroxide; polyester is one of the easier pro surfaces to treat because the stain is surface-bound.

steps: 1. Blot immediately with a clean white cloth to absorb as much liquid as possible — never rub
2. Mix 1 tbsp Dawn dish soap with 1 cup hydrogen peroxide 3%. Apply to stain
3. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat until color fades
4. Rinse with cold water to remove all cleaning solution
5. Air dry — do NOT use dryer until stain is completely gone
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 applications, or if you see any color change in the fabric. Take to a professional dry cleaner
warnings: NEVER use baking soda, ammonia, or alkaline cleaners on tannin stains — they permanently darken the color
Avoid heat until stain is fully removed

neverDo: No strong alkali at high temperature
fiberSensitivities: Oleophilic — grabs and holds oil stains tenaciously
Retains odors due to lipophilic surface
Most chemicals are safe on polyester

escalation.when: If red wine stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is red wine on polyester, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Has it been exposed to any alkaline products (baking soda, ammonia)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
